Uh, I'm the Chief Executive of TE tca, the Cancer Control, um, agency for New Zealand. I've been in this role since July of 2023. Uh, arriving here from Canada, where I held executive leadership roles at the National Cancer Control Agency in Canada.

So very similar, uh, agency at the national level, uh, in the Canadian system. I've been involved in the New Zealand Cancer system actually since 2019, uh, when I participated in the cancer at a Crossroads conference. That was in February of that year, which set the stage for the development of the cancer action plan for the 2019 29 period, and the subsequent establishment of the Cancer Control Agency.

Um, and I think it's, it's been a while since the agency has appeared before you. And so I wanted to begin by briefly outlining why New Zealand has a dedicated cancer control agency and the role that we play in the health system. Every day in New Zealand, 80 people are diagnosed with cancer. It's every day, and this year, 10,000 people will die of the disease.

Cancer is the leading cause of death in New Zealand. It's therefore only right that our health system responds to this by having an agency whose sole focus is to improve outcomes for all who face a cancer diagnosis and to prevent as many cancers as possible. For many years before the agency was established, there were growing concerns about cancer outcomes and equity in New Zealand.

Data showed that while New Zealand once performed well internationally, our outcomes, particularly the survival which measures the percent of, uh, patients diagnosed with cancer or alive after five years, had started to fall behind comparable countries. Furthermore, cancer mortality rates continue to be much higher for Maori and Pacific peoples than for paia.

This gap in outcomes was felt by those using the system, missed diagnoses, delays in treatment, and regional variations in care, all were eroding trust and causing harm. So at that point, patient advocates, clinicians, and cancer charities increasingly pressed the government to act, highlighting delays in treatment and variations in survival rates.

One of the most powerful voices there was Blair Vining. A Southland FA father diagnosed with late stage bowel cancer. Blair, alongside his wife Melissa, courageously elevated the challenges faced by patients across the system. While Blair sadly passed away, he left an extraordinary legacy.

In August of 2019, the government announced the creation of the Cancer Control Agency. Its mandate is to provide national leadership oversight and accountability for cancer control. Ensuring care is better planned, more consistent, and more equitable across the country. The agency was tasked with implementing the New Zealand Cancer Action Plan, which was a 10 year plan between 2019 and 29.

The action plan is a comprehensive framework designed to improve prevention, early detection, treatment, survivorship and pa, palliative and end of life care with equity and partnership with all players in the system. At its core, the agency has now been operating for five years. We are a departmental agency within the Ministry of Health, which means we operate independently while also benefiting from shared back office functions.

So all of our finance, hr, payroll, IT office space, et cetera, is provided by the Ministry of Health. And this structure allows us to be fiscally responsible while maintaining a specific focus on cancer. As we all know, our partners in the health system, health New Zealand, the Ministry of Health, along with others, must respond to the vast array of operational priorities each day.

The focus is rightly on the urgent and pressing needs of the health system, and that work is critical. But the great benefit of having a standalone cancer control agency is that we are not bound to the crises of the day. Instead, our role includes preparing for the challenges and opportunities of tomorrow.

And that is particularly important for cancer control, a domain that is undergoing increasingly rapid advances in screening, diagnostic treatments and research, all of which need to be anticipated and responded to. Moreover, the agency provides a critical mass of expertise supported by a large advisory network of clinicians, researchers, consumers, and others that allows us to dedicate the time necessary to develop comprehensive well thought out solutions to the challenges being faced in cancer.

As the Minister of Health's chief Advisor on Cancer, we bring a sharp focus lens to issues as they emerge, while also scanning the horizon to implement changes that will deliver results in the year ahead. One example of this is the work we completed recently developing new models of care and optimal pathways that transform how cancer care is organized, resourced, and delivered to support system sustainability and effective outcomes for patients.

So when the government invested $604 million into Pharm Act medicine's budget resulting in 33 new cancer medicines entering the system, the agency's work on models of care for systemic anti-cancer therapy, which is chemotherapy immunotherapy. Those drugs that were funded as part of the Phar Act uplift that, um, the the, the work was vital for helping the system deliver these medicines in innovative ways, both efficiently and closer to home.

There are course many more examples of the agency's impact. We have helped ensure that PET CT scans that are vital for diagnosing cancer are available to all patients within the public health system, which was not the case before. We are guiding the expansion of Linux that are radiation delivery machines, um, the infrastructure of those Linux, ensuring that it is evidence driven that the Linux are implemented in the right locations and delivered in the right way.

We are leading the development of data systems that allow these data systems allow clinicians to have real time access to critical information about their patients. We are helping the system prepare for the delivery of state-of-the-art cellular therapies that will be game changers for patients with blood cancers.

We are working closely with partners in Australia, Canada, the uk, and Europe, to benchmark variations in cancer outcomes and learn from each other on what specific capabilities improve survival and can be adopted in all of our countries. And these are just a few examples of efforts which aimed at improving outcomes for New Zealanders.

We are also an agency that is in internationally connected and respected, and this enables us to draw on expertise from across the globe, providing insights that help us tackle challenges as that we face here in New Zealand. By learning from others and sharing our own experience, we are better able to solve problems and continue to improve outcomes for people affected by cancer.

I speak from experience being from Canada. New Zealand's Cancer Control Agency is known for punching above its weight. Our approach is widely regarded as innovative and evidence driven. We are proud of this reputation and it motivates us to continue to deliver the very best for the people we serve.

I will now give you a summary of the current state of cancer in New Zealand based on the LA latest data available to us. So let's start with some good news. First, in New Zealand, the chance of dying from cancer has been declining for decades. The mortality rate, which which is the number of cancer deaths per a hundred thousand people adjusted for, uh, for age, has dropped from 145 per a hundred thousand in 2001 to 105 in 2022, that's almost a 30% decrease.

And most of that is coming from improvements in how we screen for and treat cancer. The chance of surviving cancer after five years has improved from 58% in 1998 to 68% in 2021. That translates roughly to 3000 more people alive every year in 2025.

Faster cancer treatment wait times have been nudging up towards the 90% target and the last 18 months, hundreds of millions have been invested to make many more cancer medicines publicly available to thousands of cancer patients. Other large investments have been made in expanding radiation treatment capacity through new centers in FANG and Taranaki opening this year and next with others to follow elsewhere across the MO too.

Those are wins we should celebrate, but there is much work left to do. That improvement in survival that I talked about is not happening as quickly as NPR countries. We are falling behind our international comparators incidence rates. So the, the risk of getting cancer has been dropping for decades due to steady reductions in smoking rates, but have begun to bounce up recently, likely driven by increases in other risk factors like unhealthy nutrition, physical inactivity, and excess body weight.

As shown in our recent through to diagnosis report, too many QE are getting their diagnosis of cancer in the emergency department or through an acute hospital admission when their symptoms are advanced rather than early in primary care with their GP when their cancer is more likely to be curable. Recruiting and retaining cancer clinicians remains a challenge due to internationally competitive market with workforce shortages in a number of number of areas like stem cell transplant, radiology, radiation oncology, gynecological surgery, endoscopy,

and others contributing to reduced access and increased wait times for many patients. And fa while investments in expanded navigation services for Maori are making a positive difference, gaps persist, notable among them are significant deficits in the National Travel Assistance Program, which is still falling short of meeting needs.

Although reforms are in progress, there continue to be significant gaps in the quality of our data systems and our ability to conduct clinical research at the levels needed. The state of our physical infrastructure is mixed, and a number of hospitals and cancer centers are in serious need of investment. And our need to fund increasingly expensive diagnostics and therapeutics that are coming down the pipe will continue to put major pressure on our fiscal resources.

Having highlighted the wins and remaining challenges, it's important to recognize some of the exciting opportunities on the horizon. A coordinated strategy for the elimination of cervical cancer is promising to make a cancer that continues to kill more women in low and middle income countries than any other, a rare disease in New Zealand.

We actually have a chance to eradicate the cancer in this country, and we are transforming the way we deliver care shifting from large tertiary centers to community-based delivery closer to home. And as part of that, expanding the role of specially trained nurses, radiation technologists, pharmacists, and others to take on roles that were previously done by cancer doctors.

These efforts will help us better respond to the rapidly rising demand and to improve access to needed care. We at Te te Kahu have embarked on an exciting program of work this year to move forward on all of these opportunities and more we have intentionally focused on parts of the cancer journey that have not received enough attention to date.

These include early detection and primary care support for survivors of cancer after the treatment is over and they continue to face many challenges and symptoms, early integration of palliative care for patients with advanced disease, so they don't suffer needlessly. We've also launched a new approach for system performance reports and dashboards that balance access to useful indicator data on quality and spotlight reports highlighting critical performance issues that the system needs to address.
